agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "DynaPricing is a real-time dynamic pricing system for delivery services. The CEO describes it at a high level during an all-hands meeting, providing strategic intent but leaving nearly all implementation details unspecified. This is a pre-spec, vision-stage description.",
    "bounded_context": "Pricing & Rate Management",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "DynaPricing", "definition": "The product name for the real-time dynamic delivery pricing system" },
      { "term": "Demand", "definition": "A measure of delivery request volume relative to capacity in a given area and time window — exact definition unspecified" },
      { "term": "Guardrails", "definition": "Upper and/or lower bounds on price adjustments to prevent extreme or irrational pricing outcomes" },
      { "term": "Real-time adjustment", "definition": "Price changes computed and applied dynamically as demand conditions change, not on a batch/scheduled basis" },
      { "term": "Delivery price", "definition": "The fee charged to the customer for a delivery — the output of the pricing engine" },
      { "term": "Fully automated", "definition": "No human approval required for individual price changes within the system's operating parameters" }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "Delivery prices must increase when demand is high relative to baseline/capacity",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a pricing policy. The direction and magnitude of price response to demand is a business strategy choice that leadership could change (e.g., they might later decide to absorb high demand costs to gain market share).",
      "testable_assertion": "Given demand above the 'high' threshold, the computed price must be greater than the base price for the same route/service"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "Delivery prices must decrease when demand is low relative to baseline/capacity",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Same as BR-1 — this is a pricing strategy decision. A VP could decide to hold prices flat during low demand instead of discounting.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given demand below the 'low' threshold, the computed price must be less than or equal to the base price for the same route/service"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "Price adjustments must occur in real time — prices reflect current demand conditions, not stale data",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "The freshness window is a policy choice (sub-second? 5 minutes? 15 minutes?). The CEO said 'real time' but the acceptable staleness is a tunable business parameter.",
      "testable_assertion": "The price served to a customer must be computed from demand data no older than the configured freshness threshold"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "The system must not require human approval for individual pricing decisions during normal operation",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Automation level is a policy choice. The business could later require human approval for price changes exceeding a certain magnitude.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given the system is in normal operating mode, price adjustments are applied without manual intervention"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "A price floor must exist — the delivery price must never go below a configured minimum",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "A delivery has real physical costs (fuel, labor, vehicle wear). Pricing below cost is a state integrity issue — it means every order loses money with no bound. Even aggressive discounting strategies require a floor. Without this, the system is corrupt — it would be giving away services at a loss with no limit.",
      "testable_assertion": "For any computed price P and configured floor F: P >= F, always, with no exceptions"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "A price ceiling must exist — the delivery price must never exceed a configured maximum",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a hard constraint driven by consumer protection, regulatory exposure, and brand integrity. An unbounded price is a system integrity failure — a bug or demand spike could produce a $10,000 delivery fee. The CEO explicitly mentioned guardrails. The ceiling value is configurable, but the existence of a ceiling is an invariant.",
      "testable_assertion": "For any computed price P and configured ceiling C: P <= C, always, with no exceptions"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "The pricing engine must always produce a valid price — it must never fail to return a price for a valid delivery request",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "If the pricing engine returns no price, the order cannot proceed. This is a system integrity constraint — a delivery request must always be priceable. Failure to price is equivalent to a system outage from the customer's perspective.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a valid delivery request, the pricing engine must return a numeric price within [floor, ceiling] within the configured timeout — never null, never an error propagated to the customer"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-8",
      "rule": "The price shown to the customer at booking confirmation must be the price charged — no retroactive adjustment after acceptance",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a fundamental commercial integrity constraint. In real-time pricing, the price can change between quote and booking. But once a customer accepts a price and confirms, that price is a contract. Charging a different amount is both a legal and trust violation.",
      "testable_assertion": "The price recorded at order confirmation must equal the price displayed at the moment the customer clicked 'confirm', regardless of subsequent demand changes"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-9",
      "rule": "Price changes must be monotonically responsive to demand — the system must not produce erratic price oscillations within short time windows",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Smoothing and dampening parameters are policy choices. The acceptable oscillation window and magnitude are tunable.",
      "testable_assertion": "Price changes within a configured short time window must not exceed the configured maximum oscillation threshold"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-10",
      "rule": "The system must maintain an audit trail of all price computations including the demand inputs that produced each price",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Audit depth and retention are policy decisions, but this is strongly implied by 'fully automated' — any automated pricing system in logistics needs auditability for regulatory and dispute resolution purposes.",
      "testable_assertion": "Every price computation must be logged with timestamp, demand inputs, base price, multiplier, and final price"
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Demand signal is unavailable or stale beyond threshold", "expected_behavior": "System must fall back to a configured default/base price rather than failing or producing a zero/null price. This fallback must still respect floor and ceiling." },
    { "scenario": "Demand spikes instantaneously (e.g., flash event, emergency)", "expected_behavior": "Price ceiling guardrail must activate. System should not lag behind and produce an unbounded price before the ceiling kicks in." },
    { "scenario": "Two customers request the same route within milliseconds during a price change", "expected_behavior": "Each customer must be charged the price they were shown at their individual confirmation time. Race conditions must not cause one customer to be charged the other's price." },
    { "scenario": "Demand drops to zero", "expected_behavior": "Price must not drop below the configured floor. The system must remain operational even with zero demand." },
    { "scenario": "Regulatory price cap exists in a specific jurisdiction", "expected_behavior": "Per-jurisdiction ceiling overrides must be supported. The effective ceiling is min(system ceiling, regulatory cap)." },
    { "scenario": "System deploys a new pricing model version while orders are in flight", "expected_behavior": "In-flight orders must use the price already confirmed. New quotes use the new model. No mid-order price mutation." },
    { "scenario": "Competitor pricing data feed fails", "expected_behavior": "If competitive pricing is an input to the model, feed failure must not crash pricing. Graceful degradation to internal-only pricing signals." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Direct revenue impact — dynamic pricing optimizes yield per delivery during peak demand and stimulates volume during low demand. Miscalibration risks either leaving money on the table (ceiling too low) or destroying demand (prices too aggressive). Price floor violation would mean delivering at a loss.",
    "operational": "Requires real-time demand sensing infrastructure, pricing engine with sub-second latency, and monitoring/alerting for anomalous pricing behavior. Operational burden is significant — this is a critical-path system that every order depends on.",
    "user": "Customers will experience variable pricing, which can erode trust if not communicated transparently. Surge pricing is well-understood (Uber precedent) but requires clear UX showing why the price is what it is. Customers who see wildly different prices for similar deliveries will complain.",
    "risk": "High risk surface: regulatory exposure (price gouging laws in many jurisdictions), reputational risk (viral social media posts about extreme prices), revenue risk (algorithm bugs producing $0 or $10,000 deliveries), competitive risk (if prices are consistently higher than competitors during normal demand).",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Customers", "impact": "Variable delivery costs — potential sticker shock during high demand, potential savings during low demand. Trust implications." },
      { "group": "Couriers/Drivers", "impact": "If courier pay is linked to delivery price, earnings become variable. If not linked, no direct impact but potential indirect impact via order volume changes." },
      { "group": "Finance/Revenue team", "impact": "Revenue becomes harder to forecast. Need new reporting on price distribution, yield per delivery, and demand elasticity." },
      { "group": "Customer Support", "impact": "Will receive complaints about price variability. Need training, scripts, and potentially price-match or refund policies." },
      { "group": "Legal/Compliance", "impact": "Must review pricing against price gouging regulations, consumer protection laws, and contractual obligations with enterprise customers." },
      { "group": "Sales/Enterprise accounts", "impact": "Enterprise customers with contracted rates may be excluded from dynamic pricing, or need renegotiated contracts. This is unaddressed in the CEO's description." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 30,
    "reasoning": "The CEO's description is an all-hands vision statement, not a product spec. It contains exactly zero concrete parameters (no thresholds, no demand definition, no price ranges, no jurisdictional scope, no customer segment rules). The word 'probably' before guardrails is alarming — the single most critical safety feature is described as uncertain. I have high confidence in the physical invariants I derived (floor, ceiling, price-at-confirmation integrity) because they are universal to any pricing system. But I have very low confidence in the behavioral rules because almost everything is unspecified.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Price floor must exist (physical cost constraint)",
      "Price ceiling must exist (CEO mentioned guardrails, plus regulatory/reputational necessity)",
      "Confirmed price must be honored (commercial contract law)",
      "System must always produce a valid price (availability invariant)"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Definition of 'demand' — is it order volume? Orders vs. available couriers? Geographic density? Time-windowed?",
      "Definition of 'real time' — sub-second? Minutes? What staleness is acceptable?",
      "Pricing formula — linear? Tiered? Multiplier-based? ML model?",
      "Customer segmentation — do all customers get dynamic pricing, or are enterprise/contract customers excluded?",
      "Geographic scope — per-zone? Per-city? Per-route?",
      "Guardrail parameters — what are the actual floor and ceiling values?",
      "Competitive pricing input — CEO said 'competitive' but didn't specify if competitor data is an input",
      "Courier compensation linkage — does courier pay change with dynamic pricing?"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "Guardrails (floor and ceiling) are mandatory despite the CEO saying 'probably' — I am treating this as a hard requirement because no responsible pricing system operates without them",
      "Price-at-confirmation honoring is required even though it was not mentioned — this is a universal commercial constraint",
      "The system prices individual delivery requests, not bulk/batch pricing",
      "Dynamic pricing applies to the delivery fee component, not the product/goods price"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The CEO said guardrails 'probably' exist. This is the single most important safety feature of a dynamic pricing system. 'Probably' is not acceptable for a feature that prevents $0 and $10,000 deliveries. This must be resolved to 'definitely' with concrete floor/ceiling parameters before any implementation begins.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Legal/Compliance", "Finance"],
      "options": ["Treat guardrails as mandatory (recommended)", "Make guardrails optional/configurable per deployment", "Defer guardrails to v2"],
      "recommendation": "Treat guardrails as mandatory invariants. Deferring them is reckless for a fully automated pricing system."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "No definition of 'demand' is provided. Demand could mean: raw order count, orders relative to courier capacity, orders relative to historical baseline, geographic density, or a composite signal. The entire pricing algorithm depends on this definition.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Product", "Engineering", "Data Science"],
      "options": ["Orders per time window in a zone", "Order-to-courier ratio", "Composite demand index incorporating multiple signals"],
      "recommendation": "Start with order-to-courier ratio as it captures both demand and supply. This is the standard approach in logistics dynamic pricing (similar to Uber's surge model)."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "No customer segmentation rules specified. Enterprise customers with contracted rates, loyalty program members, and retail customers likely need different pricing treatment. Applying surge pricing to contracted enterprise rates could breach existing agreements.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Sales/Enterprise accounts", "Legal/Compliance", "Customers"],
      "options": ["Apply dynamic pricing to all customers uniformly", "Exclude enterprise/contract customers", "Apply with different parameters per segment"],
      "recommendation": "Exclude customers with contracted rates from dynamic pricing. Apply to retail/on-demand customers only in v1."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No regulatory or jurisdictional analysis mentioned. Multiple jurisdictions have price gouging laws, especially during emergencies or disasters. A fully automated system that raises prices during a natural disaster (when delivery demand spikes) could violate these laws and cause severe reputational damage.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Legal/Compliance", "Customers", "Executive team"],
      "options": ["Build jurisdiction-aware ceiling overrides from day one", "Launch in unregulated markets first", "Require legal review before launch"],
      "recommendation": "Require legal review of every launch jurisdiction. Build per-jurisdiction ceiling overrides as a core feature, not an afterthought."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The relationship between delivery price and courier compensation is unspecified. If couriers earn more during surge pricing, it incentivizes supply during high demand (correct economic behavior). If courier pay is flat, surge pricing is pure margin capture with no supply-side benefit, which undermines the economic rationale for dynamic pricing.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Couriers/Drivers", "Operations", "Finance"],
      "options": ["Link courier pay to delivery price (percentage or bonus)", "Keep courier pay flat, capture margin", "Hybrid — base pay flat, surge bonus above threshold"],
      "recommendation": "Link courier compensation to surge pricing. Without supply-side incentives, dynamic pricing is just price gouging with extra steps."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
